med_suggestions.notes: Pregnancy-compatible option; a safe default when pregnancy is possible/confirmed.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Cephalosporin allergy; use caution with immediate-type severe beta-lactam allergy; adjust if renal impairment.
Immediate-type beta-lactam allergy; adjust for renal impairment.

med_suggestions.notes: Common first-line for uncomplicated cystitis when not near term and renal function adequate.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Avoid if pyelonephritis suspected (fever, flank pain); avoid if CrCl < 30 mL/min; nitrofurantoin allergy; caution near term pregnancy.

med_suggestions.notes: Single-dose alternative; generally pregnancy-compatible but confirm clinician judgment and local guidance.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Not for suspected pyelonephritis; consider availability/cost.

med_suggestions.dosing: 160/800 mg (1 DS tab) PO BID x 3 days
med_suggestions.sig: 160/800 mg (1 DS tab) PO BID x 3 days
med_suggestions.notes: Alternative when pregnancy status/trimester allows and resistance risk acceptable.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Sulfa allergy; avoid 1st trimester if possible (folate antagonist) and near term (kernicterus risk); consider local resistance; drug interactions.

note.soap:
S: 
34-year-old female with a history of renal disease presents with a 4-day history of dysuria described as burning on urination. She reports no fever, no flank pain, and no vaginal or urethral discharge. No pregnancy. Allergic to penicillin. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
No physical exam or vital signs available due to phone-only encounter.

A: 
- Working diagnosis (most likely): Uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection (cystitis) 
- Differential: 
 1. Uncomplicated cystitis 
 2. Urethritis (less likely without discharge) 
 3. Interstitial cystitis (less likely given acute onset) 
 4. Early pyelonephritis (less likely given absence of fever and flank pain) 
- Red flags considered: No fever, no flank pain, no systemic symptoms, no vaginal discharge.

P: 
  Medication suggestions withheld due to contraindication flags (renal disease / reduced kidney function, beta-lactam allergy (penicillin/PCN)). Physician review required.
- Disposition: Manage as outpatient with oral antibiotics given no systemic symptoms or red flags. 
- Plan: Recommend antibiotic therapy avoiding penicillin due to allergy. Suggested regimen: Nitrofurantoin 100 mg orally twice daily for 5 days. Alternatives include fosfomycin 3 g single dose or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (if local resistance patterns allow and no allergy). Reviewing physician will confirm appropriateness and prescribe if indicated. Encourage increased fluid intake and symptomatic relief measures. 
- Safety net: Advise patient to seek urgent care if she develops fever, flank pain, worsening symptoms, or inability to tolerate oral medications.
patient_instructions: Pain control: Acetaminophen or ibuprofen if safe for patient (avoid NSAIDs in late pregnancy; avoid if renal disease, ulcer/GI bleed risk).
Expected course: Should start improving within 24–48 hours after antibiotics.
Re-evaluation: If not improving within 48–72 hours, reassess diagnosis/resistance and consider urine culture and/or in-person evaluation.
Urgent/ED: Seek urgent care/ED for flank pain, persistent high fever/chills, vomiting/dehydration, pregnancy with systemic symptoms, inability to urinate, or worsening despite treatment.

evidence.title: IDSA Guideline: Uncomplicated Cystitis and Pyelonephritis
IDSA Guideline: Uncomplicated Cystitis and Pyelonephritis
